title: Who Wants to Be a Professor?
gist: Mary Townsend argues the professor's cultivated persona of objective, desire-less knowledge is self-destructing and never survived contact with reality; graduate school runs a bait-and-switch that converts students' love of learning into contempt and impoverishment, and large language models are the newest, purest expression of the wish to murder a professor who is already dead.

themes.theme: The professor persona and its self-destruction

insights.claim: The professor must take on the persona of 'someone who is not a person'—objective, permanently divorced from himself—because that impersonal remove is the guarantor of his claim to knowledge.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Frames academic authority as a performance of manufactured objectivity, the exact quality most exposed to automation and public distrust.
insights.quote: He must take on and so achieve the persona of someone who is not a person; for such is the guarantor of his claim to knowledge

insights.claim: As public respect for knowledge dies and a 'will to untruth' moves to the center of common consciousness, the classic story of the professor's authority (and of his destruction) loses its force.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: A cultural signal that the entire prestige basis of expert authority is eroding, relevant to declining trust in universities, media, and credentialed expertise.
insights.quote: As our respect for knowledge dies, as the will to untruth, always teasing at the edges and now at the center of common consciousness, flares and rumbles
insights.locator: But it is the remove, the dais

insights.claim: The physical architecture of almost every current classroom—one persona standing alone at one end, ideally on a dais—actively enforces the professor's isolation from students.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Institutional form encodes the hierarchy being challenged; formats that dissolve the dais (peer, self-directed, or AI-mediated learning) attack the model structurally.

insights.claim: Even though the professor job is no longer plausibly upper class and usually not even middle class, and faith in pure scholarship is worm-eaten, large numbers of people still desperately long to hold it.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Persistent oversupply of aspirants despite collapsing economics keeps academic labor cheap and exploitable, a structural feature of the higher-ed cost base.
insights.quote: the professor job no longer being plausibly upper class, and indeed, usually not even of the middle... people, many people, real ones, still desperately long to have this sort of job

insights.claim: The university depends on a laboring poor of adjuncts, contract workers, and third-appointment visitors to do essential teaching work 'just this one time more without too much pay.'
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Confirms the sector's reliance on a contingent, underpaid workforce—a fragility point for both labor unrest and reputational risk.
insights.quote: I speak of our university laboring poor, the adjunct, the contract worker, the ever-visitor on their third temporary appointment
insights.locator: too many perish for the lack

insights.claim: Teaching pays so poorly that someone good at it knows in their bones that high-end waitressing would be, monetarily, a better life.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: A vivid marker of how far academic compensation has fallen below service work, explaining talent flight and the moral hazard of the tenure lottery.
insights.quote: one looks at the classroom and knows in one’s bones that high-end waitressing would be, monetarily, a better life
insights.locator: I hadn't known if I would like teaching

insights.claim: Graduate school does not exist to inculcate a love of teaching but to enforce the persona of the professor as the truly learned man and to cultivate ambition for that persona.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: The training pipeline optimizes for status-seeking, not the teaching function universities actually sell—an internal misalignment driving the labor crisis.
insights.quote: graduate school does not exist to inculcate a love of teaching. Such an aim is far too lowly, and graduate school wishes to cultivate ambition for the highest
insights.locator: But graduate school does not exist

insights.claim: Academic formation performs a bait-and-switch that converts a student's desire for learning into desire for the persona, and then into contempt for that original desire.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Explains how the system reproduces its own gatekeeping ideology and manufactures the very contempt that hollows out its mission.
insights.quote: It is the worst sort of bait and switch, this exchange of desire for persona, the exchange of desire for contempt for desire
insights.locator: This sort of schooling teases your ambition

insights.claim: The tenured professor's remaining office is only economically possible through the calculated impoverishment of those willing to teach part-time for its own sake.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Names the direct cross-subsidy from adjunct poverty to tenured comfort—a structural dependency vulnerable to labor organizing and reform.
insights.quote: offered by someone whose time in the office is only economically possible by those who take his advice and fail, by the calculated impoverishment of those poor and foolish enough to teach
insights.locator: Consider the oddity of this advice

insights.claim: The common counsel that a student 'would already know by now' if they wanted to be a professor is self-serving and preternaturally bad advice, since it demands wanting to become a being that has no desire.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Exposes the gatekeeping logic that filters aspirants; a tell that the profession selects for conformity to persona over genuine intellectual desire.
insights.quote: If you already wanted to be the thing that has no desire, you would already know
insights.locator: Consider the oddity of this advice

insights.claim: Contempt for the contingent is so internalized that vast numbers of adjuncts at the edges of academia learn to accept their poverty as deserved fate arrived at through vice alone.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Internalized meritocratic shame suppresses collective resistance, prolonging the exploitative labor equilibrium.
insights.quote: Yet so often is this contempt internalized that vast numbers of people at the edges learn to accept it as their fate.
insights.locator: it is hard to forgive the sort of professor

themes.theme: LLMs as the death of the professor

insights.claim: The hope for the large language model—knowledge as a future public-gas-utility—is the newest and purest expression yet of the wish to murder the professor.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Reads AI adoption in education as driven by ressentiment toward expert authority, not just efficiency—reframing the demand narrative for AI knowledge tools.
insights.quote: What else is the hope for the large language model, the hope for knowledge as future-public-gas-utility, except for the newest and more pure expression of the wish to murder the professor than has ever yet been seen?

insights.claim: The professor is already dead—destroyed by desire, not by robots—because his claim to pure objectivity was never long for this world.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Argues AI is not disrupting a healthy institution but arriving at a corpse; the objectivity value proposition was already obsolete.
insights.quote: Do not look to robots to murder the professor, for he is already dead. Desire and Marlene Dietrich have been enough for him already.

insights.claim: Outsourcing the dream of objectivity to a pattern-repeating machine is just another way of dying—the same death academia already has—rather than a form of living knowledge.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: A skeptical thesis on AI-as-knowledge-utility: automating a discredited objectivity ideal reproduces its deadness rather than solving education.
insights.quote: outsourcing the dream of objectivity to a pattern-repeating machine, like Roald Dahl’s Great Automatic Grammatizator, is just another way of dying

themes.theme: Reading for pleasure as the way out

insights.claim: The only real and durable thing is a lifetime of books read for pleasure just because, which is what the soul and civilization are made of.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Positions intrinsic, non-instrumental learning as the value automation and credentialism both destroy—a counter-signal to fully utilitarian views of education.
insights.quote: this is the only thing that is real: a lifetime of books read for pleasure, just because
insights.locator: For let me tell you the truth

insights.claim: Nietzsche's distilled counsel is not to read yourself to death in your thirties, to learn desire first and quest for mastery later, and to spend real time outside your own century in order to return to it.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: An anti-burnout, anti-premature-specialization model for intellectual life, applicable well beyond academia to knowledge work under productivity pressure.
insights.quote: learn desire first and quest for mastery later; spend time outside your century, real weeks and days outside the problems of your own time the better to return

insights.claim: Eva Brann's defense of the amateur—the lover whose seriousness comes from the serious-unseriousness of love—is offered as a partial antidote to the professionalized professor persona.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: Elevates the amateur model of inquiry as a rival to credentialed professionalism, resonant with the rise of self-directed and para-academic learning.
insights.quote: the merely lover who, playfully, amats, the one who is not a profession or a professional but someone whose seriousness comes from the serious-unseriousness of love

insights.claim: The tell of the persona is a professor boasting on Twitter that he never reads a book simply for the joy of it—the death of wish set against the seething living desire of those excluded from the dais.
insights.speaker: Mary Townsend
insights.so_what: A cultural marker that elite gatekeepers have hollowed out the very passion they gatekeep, accelerating loss of legitimacy.
insights.quote: you can catch him boasting on Twitter that he never reads for pleasure
insights.locator: In our age, the persona may study
